title: Finishing handwoven cloth
subtitle: Wet-finishing, hemstitching, and twisting fringe to turn cloth off the loom into a finished textile
excerpt: Cloth off the loom is not yet a finished textile. The warp ends need securing, the weft needs setting by washing, and the dimensions will shift during wet-finishing. This technique covers the four standard finishing steps: hemstitching at the loom before cutting off, washing and blocking, pressing, and final edge treatment.
sourceNotes: Finishing handwoven cloth is foundational to weaving practice and is documented in all standard weaving manuals. Method draws on hemstitching and wet-finishing techniques standard in UK weaving guild instruction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A finishing stitch worked at the selvedge (edge) of a woven piece while still on the loom, which bundles the warp ends together and prevents the weft from unravelling after cutting off. The stitch is worked with a blunt needle and the same weft yarn, pulling groups of warp ends together and securing the last few picks of weaving.

Washing handwoven cloth in water at a temperature appropriate to the fibre, which causes the fibres to relax and bloom, the twist in the yarn to stabilise, and the dimensions of the cloth to settle to their finished size. Wet-finishing is not optional for wool cloth: unwashed wool cloth is scratchy, dimensionally unstable, and looks raw.

content.text: is done at the loom before cutting off so the last picks cannot unravel once the warp tension is released.

is done after cutting off to allow the cloth to bloom, set, and stabilise. The two steps together determine the final character of the cloth: a scarf that is not wet-finished feels stiff and commercial; a properly finished one softens and drapes.

Weave three or four picks at the end of the piece using the same weft yarn but at a slightly looser beat. Thread a blunt needle with a length of the same weft. Working from right to left along the bottom edge: pass the needle under 4 warp ends (from right to left), then back up through the same 4 warps (from left to right), then forward over those 4 warps and down into the cloth two picks up, pulling the stitch snug to bundle the 4 warp ends together. Repeat across the full width. Hemstitch the top edge before cutting off if weaving a second piece, or after cutting off if finishing the warp.

Hand-wash in lukewarm water with a small amount of wool wash. Gently press (never wring). Roll in a clean towel to remove excess moisture. Block flat to the correct dimensions and allow to dry completely before unpinning. Wool cloth shrinks and blooms during wet-finishing; allow 10-15% shrinkage in the warp direction and 5-10% in the weft direction.

Machine-wash on a 40-degree cycle with no spin (or a short gentle spin). Pull to shape while damp and press with a warm iron. Cotton and linen shrink significantly on first wash (up to 20% in both directions) and should always be machine-finished before measuring for a project that requires specific dimensions.

If the design includes fringe rather than hemstitched edges, twisted fringe is the most durable finish. Group the warp ends into bundles of 4-6 threads. Twist the bundle clockwise (Z-direction) until it kinks back on itself, then allow it to twist back against an adjacent bundle. Tie the two bundles together with an overhand knot at the fringe base. Trim the fringe ends to a level finish with sharp scissors.

Press wool cloth with a damp pressing cloth between the iron and the cloth, using a warm (not hot) iron. The steam from the pressing cloth opens the fibres without scorching. Do not glide the iron: lift and press. Press linen and cotton with a hot iron while still slightly damp.

items.problem: The hemstitch unravels after cutting off
items.cause: The stitch was not pulled snug enough or too few picks of weaving behind it
items.fix: Always weave at least 3-4 picks before hemstitching. Pull each stitch bundle snug but not so tight it puckers the cloth.

items.problem: The cloth comes out of the wash smaller than expected
items.cause: Insufficient shrinkage allowance calculated into the warp length
items.fix: Always wet-finish a sample warp before weaving a planned project. Measure the sample before and after finishing to calculate your actual shrinkage rate.
